name: kishar (horizons)
shortDescription: Kishar is a primordial goddess in Babylonian mythology, representing the horizon. She is the daughter of Tiamat and Apsu, and the mother of Anu.
description: Kishar, meaning "entirety of the earth," is a primordial goddess in Babylonian mythology. She embodies the horizon, representing the boundary between the sky and the earth. Kishar is a daughter of Tiamat, the primordial sea, and Apsu, the primordial fresh water, representing their union and the creation of the cosmos. Along with her brother and consort, Anshar (the entirety of the heavens), she is considered the mother of Anu, the sky god. Kishar's role is primarily within the Babylonian creation myth, Enuma Elish. While she doesn't have a central, active role in later myths, her significance lies in her foundational position within the cosmological narrative. She is a vital link in the chain of primordial beings that lead to the creation of the gods and the world as the Babylonians knew it. Kishar, as the horizon, represents the stability and structure that emerged from the chaos of the primordial waters, a key element in the Babylonian understanding of the universe's origins and order.

sources.title: Myths from Mesopotamia: Creation, The Flood, Gilgamesh and Others
sources.author: Stephanie Dalley

sources.title: Before Philosophy: The Intellectual Adventure of Ancient Man
sources.author: Henri Frankfort, H.A. Frankfort, John A. Wilson, Thorkild Jacobsen
